preFix.title: Replacing a double socket faceplate
preFix.subtitle: Swap a cracked or worn 13 A double socket faceplate like-for-like on an existing ring or spur circuit
preFix.excerpt: How to replace a damaged double 13 A socket outlet faceplate on an existing ring main or spur circuit, covering the live-dead-live isolation procedure, disconnecting the old faceplate, wiring the new faceplate, and testing before reinstatement.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics. BS 7671 (IET Wiring Regulations, 18th Edition). IET On-Site Guide.

content.text: Changing a socket faceplate like-for-like is Part P exempt work in England. It falls under Schedule 4 of the Building Regulations. Before touching any wire, carry out the full live-dead-live isolation check. A ring-main socket has two cable sets in the back box. A spur socket has one. Both are wired the same way, just with a different number of connections.

A new double 13 A socket faceplate to fit the existing single-gang back box. Check the back box depth: standard is 25 mm. A 35 mm box accepts most faceplates. Earth sleeving: a short length cut from any offcut of earth conductor. Warning label if the work is notifiable in your area.

Test the two-pole voltage tester against the proving unit to confirm it is working. At the consumer unit, identify and switch off the MCB for the circuit. Fit the lock-off device on the MCB. Return to the socket and test between live and neutral, live and earth. And neutral and earth with the two-pole tester: all three must read zero. Test the tester against the proving unit again to confirm it is still live and working. Only then is the circuit safe to work on.

Undo the two faceplate retaining screws. Pull the faceplate gently away from the back box: the cables inside will hold it close. Note which cable core goes to which terminal before disconnecting anything. The terminals are marked L, N, and E. Photograph the terminal connections before loosening any screw, then release each core and remove the old faceplate.
Step 3, prepare the cores for the new faceplate

Check that each conductor core has 10 to 12 mm of insulation stripped from the end. Re-strip with the wire strippers if the old ends are kinked or corroded. Any bare earth conductor must have green-and-yellow sleeving fitted before connection. On older red-and-black wiring, the red core goes to L and the black core goes to N. On modern brown-and-blue wiring, brown goes to L and blue goes to N.

Connect each core to the matching terminal on the new faceplate. Tighten each terminal screw until the core is firmly gripped and cannot be pulled out with a gentle tug. On a ring-main socket, both live cores go into the L terminal, both neutral cores into N. And both earth cores into E: the terminals accept two conductors. Fold the cables neatly into the back box and offer up the faceplate. Drive the two retaining screws until the faceplate sits flush against the wall.

Remove the lock-off device and switch the MCB back on. Plug the socket tester into the new socket: the correct-wiring indication shows all three LEDs in the correct pattern. Test both outlets. If the socket tester shows a fault, turn the MCB off, refit the lock-off. And recheck the terminal connections against the photograph taken at step 2.

A USB socket faceplate fits the same back box and is wired identically. The USB module is powered from the mains terminals on the back of the faceplate. A surface-mounted back box replaces a flush box where chasing into the wall is not possible. The faceplate fitting is the same.

Do not use a socket if the faceplate is cracked. The shutter mechanisms do not spring back after the plug is removed. Or there is any sign of burning or arcing at the terminals. A socket in this condition is a shock and fire risk and must be replaced promptly.

attrs.fix: Turn off the MCB, fit the lock-off device, and re-confirm the circuit is dead with the two-pole tester. Open the faceplate and swap the live and neutral cores to the correct terminals. Test again after reinstatement.
attrs.cause: The live and neutral cores were connected to the wrong terminals on the new faceplate.
attrs.symptom: Socket tester shows reversed live and neutral after fitting

attrs.fix: Turn off the MCB, fit the lock-off device, and open the faceplate. Check that no bare conductor is touching the back box wall. Check that earth sleeving is fitted on all bare earth cores. Refold the cables so they sit clear of the metal box sides.
attrs.cause: A conductor core is touching the metal back box, or two bare cores are bridging across terminals.
attrs.symptom: MCB trips immediately when switched back on after refitting

content.text: Step-by-step instructions for Replacing a double socket faceplate go here.
